# macOS LaunchDaemons — Privileged Root-Level Persistence

**Platform:** macos · **Category:** persistence · **Severity:** high

A root-writable /Library/LaunchDaemons/ directory lets an attacker who already has root turn a one-time compromise into a durable, reliable root foothold that survives reboot.

**MITRE ATT&CK:** [T1543.004 — Create or Modify System Process: Launch Daemon](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1543/004/)
## Description

LaunchDaemons are system-wide services defined by `.plist` files in `/Library/LaunchDaemons/`, executed as root at boot or on demand via `launchctl`. This is not itself a privilege-escalation primitive — it requires root to write there — but it is the standard way root access is converted into privileged persistence, and it is exactly the mechanism a privileged process abused via another technique (an unattended installer, a vulnerable root service, a supply-chain implant) will use to keep root access after the original vector is patched.

## Requirements

- Root or sudo access
- A payload binary or command to execute as root
## Exploitation

1. Craft a .plist specifying the payload in ProgramArguments.
2. Copy it to /Library/LaunchDaemons/.
3. Set ownership/permissions expected by launchd (root:wheel, 644).
4. Load it with `launchctl load <path>`, or let it run automatically at next boot.
## Proof of Concept

- [`poc.sh`](./poc.sh)
## Detection

| Type | Artifact | Description |
|------|----------|--------------|
| sigma | [`detections/sigma/macos_launch_daemon.yml`](./detections/sigma/macos_launch_daemon.yml) | Flags creation of .plist files under /Library/LaunchDaemons/. |
| osquery | [`detections/osquery/macos_launch_daemon.conf`](./detections/osquery/macos_launch_daemon.conf) | Periodically enumerates root LaunchDaemon plists and tracks file-event changes. |

## Mitigation

- File-integrity-monitor /Library/LaunchDaemons/ and alert on any write, not just on load.
- Require code-signed, notarized binaries for anything referenced by a LaunchDaemon.
- Treat any new LaunchDaemon as a high-severity event requiring review — legitimate software rarely needs to add one outside a signed installer.
- Restrict who can obtain root in the first place; this technique is post-exploitation, so upstream privilege controls are the real mitigation.
## References

- [MITRE ATT&CK T1543.004](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1543/004/)
- [Objective-See: Malware Persistence on macOS](https://objective-see.org/blog/blog_0x26.html)
